My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear the winds and waves may have carried it to you in pieces. I have been meaning to write to you for five long years, and I am sorry for the silence. The world has changed much since we last spoke, and I know you must have grown into a fine lady, while I have remained a simple man.

Tonight, a storm of terrible fury is coming, and I fear the waves may take me, and I am not sure if I shall survive it. I have been keeping the light all these years, and I have no doubt that I shall be able to keep it. I have been watching the skies for signs of the storm, and I have no doubt that I shall be able to keep it. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast upon the rocks, and the sea roars with a fury that makes even the stoutest heart quiver. Yet, I have not written to you in five long years, and now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning if I shall see dawn once more.

You were young when you left, and I have been a solitary sentinel upon this cliff, watching the tides and the skies, yet never the face of my own flesh and blood. I have kept your portrait upon the wall, and every evening, I have lit the lamp with a wish that you might return, that you might hear this voice once more.

But the years have passed, and I have grown old, and I have learned that time, like the sea, is not to be held. I have tried to forgive, to forget, to let go—but the storm within my heart is not so easily calmed.

I have no regrets, for I have done my duty, and I have kept the light burning, as it was meant to be. Yet, I have not been able to keep the light of your love, for it has long since gone out to sea.

And now, as the storm comes, I fear that I may not see the morning. But I write this, not in despair, but in hope that you might one day return, and that we might yet be reunited

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. It has been five long, weary years since I last spoke your name aloud, and now, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write, not for the sake of words, but for the sake of closure—of peace, if it can be found.

You were once my light, my guiding star, the reason I rose with the dawn and fell with the dusk. But time, like the sea, is relentless, and I have not been able to keep pace with you. I have grown old, and in the silence, I have forgotten how to speak.

I have watched you grow, and I have watched you depart, and in the absence of your presence, I have become a stranger in my own home. I have not spoken, not because I have nothing to say, but because I have feared that what I might say would be insufficient, or worse—unwanted.

You have not written to me, and I have not written to you. We have become two shores, drifting apart, each lost in its own tide.

But tonight is different. The storm is not merely a tempest in the sky, but a reckoning. The winds howl like a mournful dirge, and the waves crash like the heartbeat of a dying man. I have not spoken in five years, and I am not certain I shall speak again.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with a heart heavy as the storm that gathers on the horizon. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown like the dunes that stretch beyond the sea. I have kept the lighthouse, as you once asked me to, and I have kept it well—though not without sorrow.

The sea has been kind to me, though it has taken much from me. I have seen the moonlight dance upon the waves, and I have heard the whispers of the wind. But I have not heard your voice, nor felt your touch. I have kept the lamp burning, as you wished, and I have tended the light for all who pass by, yet I have not seen your face in the glow.

Tonight, a storm is coming—a great and terrible storm. The sky is dark, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have seen such storms before, and I know they are not to be trifled. The sea may rise, and the waves may crash against the rocks, and the lighthouse may tremble. But I have stood through them all, and I have endured.

I do not know if I shall survive this night, Eleanor. I have no fear of the sea, for I have known its moods and its wrath. But I do fear for you, for I have not seen you in years, and I have not heard your voice.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of five long years of silence. The storm is upon us now, a tempest that seems to howl with the sorrow of the sea itself. I have not spoken for so long that I fear I have forgotten the sound of my own voice. But tonight, as the wind howls through the cracks in the stone walls and the waves crash like the beating of a thousand hearts, I find myself compelled to speak—though I do not know if my words will reach you, or if you will even read them.

I have not spoken, but I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten your laughter, the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the lighthouse in the dark, or how you used to sneak into the tower at night to watch the sea from the top. I have not forgotten the way you would run your fingers along the edge of the lantern glass, whispering secrets to the sea as if it could hear them. I have not forgotten your voice, though I have not heard it for five years.

I have not spoken, but I have not ceased to love. I have not ceased to watch the horizon, to chase the dawn, to be the keeper of this place, this promise of light in the dark. And yet, I have not been able to keep the light of you in my heart.
